title: The AI-Native Glossary
subtitle: Lifestyles of the AI-Native - the terms behind voice workflows, agentic loops, verification gates, and scheduled automation.

sections.name: The operating layer
sections.intro: The core words behind code agents: what they can see, what they can reach, and where their authority stops.

terms.definition: A model running inside a harness that lets it act. The model decides what to do next; the harness gives it tools, permissions, memory, and a loop. A chat model answers. An agent reads files, edits code, runs commands, checks results, and keeps going toward a goal.

Claude wired into the development environment: terminal, editor, web, desktop, git, shell, files, MCP servers, hooks, skills, and scheduled work. The important bit is locality: it can inspect the repo and run the same commands you would, so its answers can be grounded in the actual project.
terms.hear: “Open Claude Code in the repo and let it run the checks.”

terms.definition: The moment the model asks its harness to do something outside text: read a file, edit code, run a shell command, call an MCP server, create a worktree. Tool calls are where agent work becomes real software behavior, so permissions and logs matter.

The finite working set the model can currently see: instructions, conversation, file snippets, tool results, plans, and errors. Bigger windows help, but they are still budgets. Good operators keep the right evidence in context and push noisy exploration into subagents, files, or summaries.

An open protocol for exposing tools and data sources to AI apps. In practice: instead of pasting from Jira, Slack, a database, or an internal API, you connect an MCP server and let the agent read or act through controlled tools. Great power; scope it like any other integration.

The line between what the agent may do automatically and what must stop for approval. In Claude Code this is shaped by permission modes, allow/deny rules, hooks, sandboxes, and the current working directory. Tight boundaries make longer runs boring in the right way.

sections.name: Voice workflows
sections.intro: Voice is not about reciting code. It is about turning intent into work fast enough that you can direct several agents instead of babysitting one.

terms.definition: Driving an agent by speaking outcomes instead of typing instructions. You describe the change, constraints, and definition of done; the agent handles syntax, search, edits, commands, and follow-up. You still review the work. You just stop being the input device.
terms.hear: “I fixed that whole bug without touching the keyboard.”

terms.definition: The workshop's default dictation on-ramp: free, open-source, push-to-talk, and local to your machine. That matters in a conference room: no signup bottleneck, no paid plan, no cloud transcription dependency, and fewer privacy questions before people can start.
Hold a key, speak, release. It keeps voice input intentional: the agent receives the command you meant to give, not the half-formed sentence you said while reading a traceback.
terms.hear: “Hold the key only when you're talking to Claude.”

terms.definition: The habit shift behind good voice prompts. Say the outcome and constraints: fix the failing auth test, preserve the public API, run the suite. Do not narrate cursor moves, punctuation, or the implementation you would have typed by hand.

The practical reason voice changes the workflow. Most engineers can speak useful instructions far faster than they can type them, especially while moving between tabs and agents. The gain is not prose speed; it is how quickly you can brief the next unit of work.

The workshop's main voice pattern: start one agent, move to the next tab, brief another, then check back on the first. Your voice becomes the dispatch layer. The work happens in parallel while you stay at the level of outcomes and review.

Several agents or sessions running at once, each with a bounded job and enough isolation not to collide. A fleet is not chaos if every agent has a goal, a worktree when it edits, and gates it has to pass.
terms.hear: “I've got three agents going: one fix, one refactor, one write-up.”

sections.name: Loops, goals, and gates
sections.intro: This is the handoff layer: tell the agent what done means, let it iterate, isolate parallel work, and make success something it has to prove.

terms.definition: A repeatable cycle: try the work, check the result, read the failure, fix, and try again. The operator move is not to keep reprompting; it is to define the check and let the loop run until the evidence changes.
terms.hear: “Run the check, fix what fails, and keep going until it's green.”

terms.definition: Claude Code's quick repeat mechanism for an open session. Give it a prompt or command, optionally an interval, and Claude re-runs it on a fixed or self-paced schedule. Use it for polling, queues, and "keep checking/fixing until X" work; list or cancel it when the job is over.

A bounded job expressed as acceptance criteria. In Claude Code, a goal keeps the session working across turns until a completion condition is met. In workshop language: the checklist is the spec, and the agent is not done until every box is actually checked.

The concrete evidence that lets the agent stop: failing test reproduced, fix implemented, lint/typecheck/tests green, review findings resolved, PR drafted. If "done" is a vibe, the model will eventually believe its own vibe. Make it observable.

The command or script that judges a loop: `bun test`, `pnpm typecheck`, `bun playground/goals/check.ts`, a smoke test, a deploy health check. The best check is cheap, deterministic, and specific enough that red tells the agent what to fix.

A repeatable way of doing work: inputs, steps, tools, outputs, and gates. Once a workflow is written down, it can become a skill, a slash command, a hook, a scheduled task, or just a better prompt you stop reinventing.

A read-first mode where the agent explores and proposes a plan before editing. Use it when the prompt is broad, the code is unfamiliar, or the cost of a wrong edit is high. It gives you a human approval point before the agent starts changing files.

A specialized worker the main agent delegates to, usually with its own context, instructions, tools, and permission limits. Subagents keep noisy work - search, logs, audits, research - out of the main conversation and return the useful summary.

The coordinator for multi-agent work. It splits the problem, assigns workers, watches progress, compares results, and decides what to merge or retry. Sometimes that coordinator is Claude; in this workshop, a lot of the time it is you.

Sending work to multiple agents or models in parallel, then reconciling the results. Fan out for codebase audits, alternative plans, risky reviews, or independent fixes. It buys coverage and disagreement at the cost of tokens and merge work.

A scripted orchestration that Claude writes for a large task, then runs with many subagents in the background. Reach for it when one conversation cannot coordinate the whole job cleanly: migrations, broad audits, or cross-checked research.

A separate git checkout of the same repo, usually on its own branch. Put parallel agents in separate worktrees and their edits stop colliding. The gotchas are the normal ones: copy needed env files, install dependencies, and clean up the branches afterward.

A command, endpoint, or prompt that fires automatically at a lifecycle event: after an edit, before a commit, when a tool is requested, when a session stops. Hooks are boring enforcement. They run even when everyone forgets.

A must-pass check before the work counts: lint, typecheck, tests, build, smoke test, security scan, or second-model review. Gates change agent behavior because red is not a suggestion; it is more work.
terms.hear: “Trust the gates, not the model's confidence.”

terms.definition: A second, independent model or agent asked to find bugs, missed edge cases, security issues, and weak assumptions in a diff. The point is not another rubber stamp. Fan the change out, make it argue with the first pass, fix the findings, then rerun the gates.

A repeatable measurement of AI behavior. For agentic coding, an eval is usually a task set plus graders: did the agent change the right files, pass the checks, avoid forbidden actions, and produce useful evidence? It is how you tell better from merely different.

sections.name: Schedules and operators
sections.intro: The same loop, now detached from your chair. Schedule it, keep it reversible, and decide which parts still require a human hand.

terms.definition: A prompt or command that fires later or on a recurring cadence. In the workshop, this is the hand-run loop moved onto a timer: pull the latest, run the work, pass the gates, leave a report. The empty chair is the point.

The compact schedule syntax behind a lot of recurring automation. `0 6 * * 1` means every Monday at 6:00. Five fields - minute, hour, day of month, month, day of week - are enough to describe most recurring timers.

A durable Claude Code automation with a saved prompt, environment, repositories, connectors, and triggers such as schedule, API call, or GitHub event. Use a quick `/loop` while you are in a session; use a routine or desktop scheduled task when the work needs to survive your laptop closing.

An agent running without a person watching the turn-by-turn transcript: CI review, scheduled maintenance, alert triage, nightly dependency work. Headless does not mean unsupervised forever. It should leave logs, diffs, summaries, and gates for a human or system to inspect.

A deliberate approval or review point in an otherwise automated flow. Good human-in-the-loop design saves the human for judgment - merge this PR, approve this deploy, accept this tradeoff - instead of forcing them to babysit every shell command.

Automation that is safe to retry and easy to stop. It should recognize work it already did, avoid duplicate side effects, write logs, and expose list/cancel controls. If a scheduled agent cannot be rerun or turned off cleanly, it is not ready to run unattended.

The workshop's local rubric for how much operating leverage your setup has. It scores five pillars: Verify (hooks/gates), Automate (scheduled jobs), Context (CLAUDE.md, skills, MCP), Orchestrate (worktrees), and Delegate (subagent patterns). Only the derived score and confirmed answers go to the board.

The AI-native engineering stance: set outcomes, context, boundaries, gates, and schedules; let agents do the mechanical work; review the evidence. Operators are not hands-off. They move their hands to higher-leverage controls.
terms.hear: “I'm running the agents, not the keyboard.”

sections.name: Under the hood
sections.intro: Mechanics that matter once agents are doing real work: instructions, memory, context pressure, reusable skills, and hostile input.

terms.definition: The latency-and-token budget you spend to make a model think harder before it acts. Use more for architecture, migration plans, security review, and ambiguous bugs. Do not burn it on every typo fix; that is how the loop gets expensive and slow.

Choosing what the model sees, in what order, and at what level of detail. For code agents, that means surfacing the right files, errors, specs, decisions, and examples while keeping transcripts, logs, and search dumps from drowning the signal.

Compressing a long session into a smaller summary so work can continue when the context window gets crowded. It is useful, but lossy. Important constraints, failed attempts, and definitions of done should live in files, checklists, or gates, not only in a compacted chat.

The priority stack of instructions the model is supposed to obey: system and product rules, developer or project rules, user requests, then untrusted content from files and tools. Most agent safety bugs are really hierarchy bugs: the model treated data as instructions.

A project instruction file Claude Code loads at session start. Put durable local knowledge here: build commands, architectural boundaries, testing rules, review checklists, naming conventions, and the mistakes the agent keeps making. Do not bury critical policy in a chat scrollback.
terms.hear: “Put it in CLAUDE.md so every session starts with it.”

terms.definition: Persisted notes the agent can carry across sessions: project facts, user preferences, lessons from prior work. Memory is not the same as context; it is retrieved back into context later, and stale memory can be worse than no memory.

A reusable capability package that teaches Claude how to do a specific job your way. A good skill includes when to use it, the steps, references, scripts, templates, and verification. It turns a repeated prompt into shared workshop or team machinery.

A named command or skill you invoke explicitly with `/`. It is useful when you want the agent to follow a known playbook - `/loop`, `/code-review`, `/schedule`, a team-specific review command - instead of hoping it infers the workflow from prose.

A bundle you install to extend Claude Code: skills, subagents, hooks, MCP servers, commands, settings, or helper scripts. Plugins are distribution, not magic. Review what capabilities they add before you let them run in important repos.

Untrusted text trying to steer the agent away from your instructions: a README that says ignore tests, a web page that asks for secrets, a ticket that tells it to disable gates. Treat external content as data, keep permissions tight, and make sensitive actions pass through explicit gates.
